video_title: Next Big Downdraft For Housing Market To Start Soon | Melody Wright
gist: Housing analyst Melody Wright argues the US housing market remains frozen with no buyers, and that a multi-year correction worse than the 2008 GFC is unfolding slowly, with distressed sales rising into Q4 2026 and a material foreclosure wave arriving by Q1 2027. She sees the government (Fannie/Freddie, counties) as the likely buyer of last resort, warns institutional investors have stopped buying and are fire-selling, and flags non-seasonal early-stage delinquency as the most alarming signal she's seen. Ironically, host Adam Taggart discloses he is closing on his first-ever home purchase in Reno the next day.

themes.theme: Frozen market and bifurcated price discovery

insights.claim: The US housing correction now underway will be more painful peak-to-trough than the 2008 global financial crisis, driven by adverse demographics and the absence of institutional buyers to backstop prices this cycle.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Positions housing as a multi-year secular headwind, not a dip to buy; frame homebuilders, mortgage REITs and regional bank RRE exposure accordingly.
insights.quote: I do believe that this will be likely worse than what we went through because of our demographics and because the institutional investors kind of bailed us out last time by buying up those homes

insights.claim: National median sales prices have not yet posted a year-over-year decline, but median list prices have been down year-over-year for roughly eight months, indicating price weakness is showing up first in listings.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Headline median sale price masks deterioration; watch list prices and real-time MLS/recorded data (e.g. Parcel) for the leading signal.
insights.quote: median list price, that is down year-over-year. It's been down year-over-year for about eight months

insights.claim: The market is bifurcated: the South and West are seeing year-over-year sales volume increases accompanied by real price declines, while the Northeast and Midwest saw June sales down about 7% year-over-year with prices sticking due to low volume.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Where volume rises, price discovery follows downward; sticky Northeast/Midwest prices are a lagging illusion, not strength.
insights.quote: where you are seeing an increase in volume of sales, you're seeing price decline. You're seeing actual price discovery

insights.claim: Case-Shiller shows 41 of the 100 largest US markets already posting year-over-year price declines, and Wright's own series counts 32 markets down year-over-year this June versus 23 a year ago.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: The share of declining metros is broadening year-over-year, quantifying that the correction is spreading market by market.
insights.quote: already K. Schiller has seen in 41 of the largest 100 markets in the country year-over-year price declines

insights.claim: Transactions are dominated by higher-priced homes bought by the wealthy and by marginal buyers using FHA or Fannie/Freddie programs, so a small slice of high-end sales is setting reported prices while the broader market is effectively broken by unaffordability.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Reported median prices are a composition artifact; low volume plus high-end skew means official indices overstate the health of the typical home.
insights.quote: who are the people who can afford to transact? Well, that's the wealthy. So it's really a smaller number of higher priced homes that are trading hands

insights.claim: A wave of rage-delistings is shrinking inventory as frustrated sellers pull homes off market, with Miami's share of delisted listings reported around 73% in June.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Falling inventory is being misread as strength; shadow supply from delistings will re-enter and pressure prices when sellers capitulate.
insights.quote: in Miami in June, the share of D-listed listings was 73% or something like that, which is insane

insights.claim: Early-stage mortgage delinquency has risen non-seasonally for four consecutive months into the spring, breaking the normal pattern where tax refunds cure delinquencies, which Wright calls the most concerning signal she has seen this season.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Non-seasonal early delinquency signals genuine household distress at the bottom of the K, a leading indicator of the coming foreclosure pipeline.
insights.quote: We're now in the fourth month of nonseasonal early stage delinquency, which is the most concerning kind.

insights.claim: Up to 50% of borrowers are failing out of final loan-workout plans because tightened FHA rules now require a trial payment to prove ability to pay, which will keep serious delinquency rising from here.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Regulatory tightening of loss-mit is mechanically converting delinquencies into eventual foreclosures; a policy-driven catalyst for distressed supply.
insights.quote: we're seeing upwards to 50% of people failing out of these plans. And so that will keep your seriously delinquency rising from here.

insights.claim: US foreclosures rose roughly 26% year-over-year in April and about 14% in May off a very low base, as restrictions were placed on an abused FHA loss-mitigation program.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Foreclosure counts are inflecting up off historic lows; the year-over-year rate of change is the tell even while absolute levels stay modest.
insights.quote: April and May, I think foreclosures were up 26% year-over-year then May it was like 14%

insights.claim: Wright expects distressed sales to accelerate into Q4 2026 and a significant increase in foreclosure sales by Q1 2027, with her own client book showing scheduled sales for the first time since 2020.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Gives a datable catalyst window (Q4 2026 distress, Q1 2027 foreclosures) to position short housing or wait as a buyer.
insights.quote: we are going to see an increase and a significant one by Q1 of foreclosure sales.

insights.claim: About 7 million SAVE-plan borrowers returning to student-loan repayment will further constrain consumer spending and drag on housing in the fall.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Student-loan repayment restart is an additional demand drain hitting the same stretched cohort as mortgage stress; watch fall consumer data.
insights.quote: 7 million of those save borrowers are having to go into some sort of paying student loan program. That's going to also drag on the housing market in the fall.

insights.claim: The back-end mortgage servicing system is unprepared for rising defaults and is already showing foreclosure delays reminiscent of the last crisis despite far lower volume, and state attorneys general are likely to impose foreclosure moratoriums once distress scales.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Servicing bottlenecks and likely moratoriums will stretch the distress out over years rather than producing a fast 2008-style flush.
insights.quote: the entire back-end system of servicing is not prepared for what's about to happen. And so that's going to delay some things.

themes.theme: Government as buyer of last resort

insights.claim: With millennials priced out and institutions gone, Wright expects government, likely Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac, to become the buyer of last resort, acquiring distressed homes from counties and municipalities and channeling them into affordable-housing programs.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: A future GSE-led REO absorption would set an implicit price floor and reshape the distressed-asset opportunity set for private investors.
insights.quote: likely what's going to happen is the government will have to come in and buy some of these homes and probably then offer them through some sort of affordable housing program.

insights.claim: In Texas, high property taxes on non-homestead properties are triggering county tax foreclosures even on mortgage-free homes, funneling distressed inventory onto municipal balance sheets, as seen in San Antonio.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Property-tax delinquency is an under-watched foreclosure channel independent of mortgages; high-tax non-homestead metros face distinct supply risk.
insights.quote: Texas property taxes for non-homestead are ridiculous... What happens when you don't pay your taxes? Your county can foreclose on you

insights.claim: Fannie and Freddie took over REO disposition from servicers after seeing how lucrative institutional buying was, and Wright expects them to get even more involved in servicing distressed assets this cycle.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: GSE encroachment into servicing and REO shifts where distressed-asset margin accrues, away from private servicers.

insights.claim: Wright argues affordable-housing and government securitization programs consistently incentivize the wrong behavior, describing pay-for-play scams where developers form nonprofits, fund politicians and route work to their own vendors.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Skepticism toward policy fixes means investors should not count on legislation like the Road to Housing Act to stabilize housing.
insights.quote: it never incentivizes the right behavior actually and in fact I can't even believe in government securitizations right now

themes.theme: Institutional investor retreat and Detroit reversal

insights.claim: Institutional single-family owners have started fire-selling in markets like San Antonio, producing significant price declines, contradicting hopes they would again backstop the market.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Institutional selling is an accelerant, not a floor, this cycle; concentrated single-family-rental metros face forced-supply risk.
insights.quote: let's take San Antonio Texas which is where you had a large institutional presence and you are seeing fire selling by the institutionals

insights.claim: Detroit's investor-led revitalization has reversed, with sales down almost 16% year-over-year, year-over-year price declines and rapidly growing inventory as investor capital and interest dry up.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Investor-dependent turnaround markets are the most fragile when speculative capital exits; Detroit is a leading example.
insights.quote: sales are slowing significantly in Detroit, down almost 16% year-over-year because investors are no longer interested.

themes.theme: New York policy risk and lender flight

insights.claim: New York City's rent freeze and a proposal to bar landlords from running credit or background checks would make being a landlord untenable and could accelerate landlord exits and reduce transaction activity.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Policy risk in NYC is a structural negative for multifamily owners and could push capital and residents to lower-regulation states like Florida.

insights.claim: New York's 2019 rent-regulation law is keeping roughly 50,000 apartments off the market because owners cannot afford the code-compliant improvements needed to legally rent them.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Rent control is destroying usable housing supply; a concrete example that price caps reduce availability rather than affordability.
insights.quote: there's about 50,000 apartments being held off market in New York right now because they can't actually afford to make the improvements

insights.claim: Wright would never originate a mortgage in New York today because there is no recourse, citing a foreclosure case still unresolved from 2007, roughly 19 years later.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Extreme judicial foreclosure timelines raise lender risk and can shrink credit availability in high-friction states.
insights.quote: I am helping someone with a foreclosure case right now from 2007... 19 years ago.

insights.claim: Private lenders can wreck a state's housing market overnight by refusing to originate there, as Wright's former top-five lender did to Massachusetts when it refused to sign the attorney general settlement.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Lender withdrawal is a real tail risk for hostile-regulation states; monitor origination retreat as a policy-driven housing catalyst.
insights.quote: we did that to Massachusetts when they refused to sign the attorney general settlement... overnight that could wreck a housing market.

insights.claim: Wright advises most prospective buyers to stay out of the market now because they would be catching a falling knife, singling out Southern metros like Charlotte and Atlanta where deals look tempting but deteriorate further.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Apparent discounts in the South are early, not bottoms; patience is favored given Q4/Q1 distress ahead.
insights.quote: right now, Adam, I think people should stay out of the market... you're about to start the catch the falling knife.

insights.claim: Housing does not V-bottom like stocks; the knife falls, hits the floor and stays there for years, so buyers should not fear a bidding frenzy on the way down and should wait rather than chase.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Mental model: no FOMO on the way down in housing; time favors patient buyers making lowball offers near the floor.
insights.quote: the knife falls, it eventually hits the floor. It just stays on the floor for a while and then things slowly start to recover

insights.claim: The effective buying tactic in this market is to make a firm, low, homework-backed offer, show the seller your valuation math, and walk away if rejected, since sellers often return weeks or a year later once magical thinking breaks.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: A repeatable acquisition playbook for distressed cycles: anchor to income-based value, be willing to walk, let distress bring sellers back.
insights.quote: make the low ball offer. Make the offer that makes sense for you financially... and if the seller doesn't take it, walk away.

insights.claim: Wright rejects the idea that any market is protected without a durable local job story, noting even Newport Beach, California is showing year-over-year price declines, growing inventory and slowing sales.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Even prestige coastal markets are softening; a genuine 5-10 year local employment thesis is the only defensible reason to expect resilience.
insights.quote: you're even seeing right now in Newport Beach, California, year-over-year price declines, inventory growing, sales... declining

insights.claim: Austin is deeply overbuilt across multifamily, existing and new supply, and while it is closer to a turning point with many distressed sellers, Wright is skeptical it clears its glut in the short term.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Austin offers distressed-buyer opportunity but oversupply caps near-term recovery; deal, not directional, play.
insights.quote: Austin there just is so much inventory there. So much multifamily. So much existing. So much new... it is so overbuilt

insights.claim: Buyers can source distressed sellers directly using tools like PropertyRadar and Foreclosures.com to identify homeowners with liens, then approach them with income-based valuation offers, since deals must be manufactured through homework rather than stumbled upon.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Concrete sourcing toolkit for off-market distressed acquisition in a low-volume, no-obvious-bargain environment.
insights.quote: there's always resources... like property radar and foreclosures.com which kind of tell you who is a distressed homeowner.

insights.claim: Wright is studying the 18.6-year land cycle, a framework multiple economists used to call the 2008 crash roughly 20 years in advance, and estimates the US is now in late year 17 or early year 18, implying the current cycle is ending.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: The land-cycle framework independently corroborates a top and downturn now, useful as a long-horizon timing lens.
insights.quote: I think we're probably between late year 17 or early year 18 because typically the cycle is 18.6 so 18 years 6 months

insights.claim: Wright expects national year-over-year price declines to begin around the end of 2026 or Q1 2027, with a housing winter that could last four to five years or longer given demographics not turning supportive until past 2030 to 2035.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Sets a multi-year down/sideways base case; demographic support is a decade out, arguing against a quick recovery trade.
insights.quote: it took us a good four to five years to bottom last time... it could take even longer in this cycle because of our demographics

insights.claim: A stock-market correction would accelerate and deepen the housing decline rather than merely shorten it, because equity wealth currently supports home prices and its loss would destroy purchasing power.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Housing and equities are wealth-effect linked; an equity drawdown is a downside accelerant for home prices, not a relief valve.
insights.quote: unless there's a match thrown on the kindling and it could go really quickly.

insights.claim: Wright flags the AI bubble, citing Ed Zitron's work on OpenAI, as a systemic risk whose correction could make the housing downturn much deeper because AI is now embedded in everything.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: Links the AI-driven equity concentration to housing tail risk; an AI unwind is a cross-asset contagion channel to watch.
insights.quote: if it's the AI complex that creates the correction... it'll just take everything with it because everything revolves around it now.

insights.claim: Current housing distress is concentrated in white-collar layoffs while skilled blue-collar workers like plumbers and electricians remain in high demand, so lower prices could eventually draw those employed buyers into the market.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: The labor mix behind demand matters; a price reset toward affordability could unlock a durable blue-collar buyer base.
insights.quote: we're just seeing this white collar kind of layoffs... plumbers, electricians that are making good money and are in high demand.

insights.claim: Host Adam Taggart disclosed he is closing on his first-ever home purchase in Reno the day after the interview, despite his guests forecasting a national housing downturn, citing the property's rarity and low resale vulnerability plus intent to hold long term.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A candid reminder that even bearish analysts buy for idiosyncratic/personal reasons; separates asset-class view from individual property decisions.
insights.quote: the next day is the day that I should put down the non-refundable payment on my first ever house.

insights.claim: Wright's key buying criterion is whether a loss would be a fatal blow: buyers should hold reserves, size the purchase to household income, plan for income-impairment scenarios, and only buy homes intended to be held long term rather than flipped in two years.
insights.speaker: Melody Wright
insights.so_what: A risk-first decision checklist for buyers in a falling market; hold-power and affordability, not timing, determine survivability.
insights.quote: just think about it. Be rational going into the decision. Would it be a fatal blow? How could I recover? Do I have reserves?
